scrolls.book_title: Baba Johani — Life Events and Sacred Mission
verses: CHAPTER 1: THE PORTENTS BEFORE THE BIRTH — In the days before his appearing, when the land was called Gandanzara, and the homestead of Mugwambi stood firm upon the soil, a sign came that was not of men.
For a stranger appeared, not known by name, nor traced by clan, yet his presence carried the weight of heaven.
He came as one sent, like a vision clothed in flesh, and the elders did not question him, for awe restrained their tongues.
An ox was taken and slaughtered, and its flesh was consumed in a single day, a thing not done among the people.
And the bones were not scattered, but were gathered and buried at Gandanzara, as one buries a vow before God.
From that day, the womb of Mai Saziso Efi was visited, and conception followed the sign.
And while the child was yet hidden, wonders multiplied.
For the mother dreamed continually of a man clothed in white, descending from above.
In his hand was a cord of three colors, shining as a rainbow, which he lowered to her womb and lifted again to heaven.
And when three months had passed, the child moved with strength, and the womb danced, and fear mixed with reverence filled her heart.
And the voice instructed her to pray in the name of Jesus, and she obeyed.
Thus was the ground prepared before the seed broke the earth.
CHAPTER 2: THE DAY OF BIRTH AT GANDANZARA (1 OCTOBER 1914) — On the first day of October, in the year 1914, the child was born at Gandanzara, in the homestead of Mugwambi, Zimbabwe.
AUTHORITATIVE DATE: Baba Johani Masowe was born on 1 October 1914. He was NOT born in 1933. The year 1933 marks a different event entirely.
And they named him Shoniwa, not knowing how many names would follow him.
From his infancy, his body did not behave as other bodies.
For he would fall as one struck down, and lie as though lifeless, then rise again without warning.
Fear came upon those who watched him, for life clung to him differently.
Sickness did not claim him, yet weakness visited him often, as though his spirit carried more than his flesh could bear.
And it was said among the elders, This child walks near the boundary.
CHAPTER 3: THE CHILD WHO WALKED ALONE — As he grew, he did not follow the paths of other children.
School did not hold him, nor games bind him.
He fled instead to the hills, especially to the hill called Shena, near Mugwambi's place.
There he broke stones, shaped objects, and remained alone from sunrise to sunset.
When called, he returned silently, carrying the works of his hands.
And many said, What spirit teaches this child where no man stands?
For he was often found where no path was made.
CHAPTER 4: THE CHILD TAKEN TO THE COURT ABOVE — At about five years of age, the child testified later that he was taken.
Not carried by men, nor led by beasts, but lifted to the court of heaven.
And there he saw others, and heard matters not spoken among the living.
From that time, he knew that his journey was not bound to earth alone.
And he walked knowing he would return and depart many times.
CHAPTER 5: THE NAMES THAT FOLLOWED HIM — As the years passed, names gathered around him like garments.
He was called Shoniwa, Sixpence, Peter, Eria, Johani, Mangemba, and Harahwa.
And he declared that four of these were given from heaven, and three from men.
And the name Johani would stand above the rest.

On 10/05/1966, Baba Johani spoke of his lineage: My grandfather was called Mutambararo. He was given the chieftainship but did not complete a month before he died. Masedza succeeded him. Masedza bore my father Jack. Jack bore me, Shoniwa.
CHAPTER 6: THE DESCENT OF THE HOLY SPIRIT AT NORTON (1 OCTOBER 1932/1933) — On the first day of October 1932, while at Norton on Maitland Farm, the Voice of God came upon him.
He was struck by lightning (rupango/mheni) while riding a bicycle from the east, and fell to the ground.
For sixteen days he lay unable to speak, his body seized by the power from above.
Samson Mativera, Mazhambe, and Cheurombo carried him and cared for him in their dwelling.
One night he was found outside though the door had been barred with planks.
At dawn, a voice was heard from heaven calling: Sixpence, arise!
And he was given his commission at Chipukutu, on the hill of Marimba, at Norton.
He was nineteen years old when the Holy Spirit descended upon him, for he was born in 1914.
CHAPTER 7: THE FIRST ARRESTS AND RELEASE — After the Spirit came upon him, Baba Johani was seized at Norton.
He was taken to Norton Police Station, then transferred to Chegutu (Hartley), then to Kadoma, then to Harare, then to Marondera, then to Rusape, and finally to Buhera.
These are the seven places of his suffering in the early days.
At his hearing, the judges asked the people, Does this man take money when he heals or preaches? And the people answered with one voice, No.
His case was judged at the Anglican Cathedral in Harare, before Archbishop Cripps, Bishop Lulu from America, Father O'hea of Kutama, and the Government.
They said, If he speaks the word of God and takes no money, let him do his work. And he was released.
CHAPTER 8: THE MOUNTAIN OF MARIMBA — After his release, Baba Johani went to Gomo reMarimba (Marimba Hill) at Norton.
There the people gathered in great numbers.
There the staff (tsvimbo) that sang was first heard.
His name spread across the land.
At Chipukutu in Hunyani River, he cast his fishing line into the water, and the fish bit like bees.
At night, the stars appeared to dance, and even the trees were said to sing.
Some said, This is Chaminuka the prophet returned. Others said, No, this one walks on water and does not sink; Chaminuka drowned. This one does not play mbira; his songs are of the church.
CHAPTER 9: NYAMWEDA AND CHIVERO — Baba Johani walked from Marimba toward Nyamweda and Chivero.
With him were witnesses including Samson Mativera, Mazhambe, Sylvester, and Enock Chakandamina.
He prayed wherever he met a madman (harahwa), for he said he was sent to heal them.
At Nyamweda, he sang continually: Kurwadza nedambudziko zvakanaka, nhasi ugare nesu. Mununuri Jesu wauya nhasi, ugare nesu.
A policeman named Chida came to arrest him and was struck blind. After Baba prayed, his sight returned.
Another policeman named Banda testified everywhere to what he had seen.
CHAPTER 10: ZAKA AND MASVINGO (1939) — In 1939, Baba Johani traveled to Zaka in Masvingo Province.
A policeman named Edward Japajapa of Dowa in Dewedzo came to arrest him.
When Japajapa beat the people with his sjambok, Baba Johani rescued a young man named Richard Maiunda.
Witnesses saw Baba Johani fly like an eagle (gondo), carrying the young man by the hand to the top of the hill.
This miracle at Zaka became known throughout the land.
CHAPTER 11: BULAWAYO AND GWERU — From Masvingo, Baba Johani traveled to Bulawayo with Muvhangeri Saizi, Samuel Chinyanga, Sailos Nherera, Muparidzi John Mupani, and Topias.
At Bulawayo and Leighwoods, he found rest from persecution for a time.
There were added to the fellowship: Mai Megi, Mai Efi Manyathi, Mai Rose, and Mai Rhoda Kutsanzira.
From Bulawayo he went to Gweru, where he met Muparidzi George Marondera.
He left his tools there, for he was a carpenter who made chairs and tables.
CHAPTER 12: PORT ELIZABETH, SOUTH AFRICA (06/08/1951) — On 6 August 1951, Baba Johani arrived at Port Elizabeth in South Africa.
The church grew rapidly among the workers and migrants there.
He established a strong community that would continue for decades.
CHAPTER 13: BABA SPEAKS OF HIS DEATH (20/08/1953) — On 20 August 1953, Baba Johani first spoke openly about his own death.
He said, My time will come, and those with ears will understand.
Yet he did not hurry, nor did he tremble, for he had seen beyond the veil.
CHAPTER 14: THE DREAM OF THE WHITE CLOTH (06/10/1954) — On 6 October 1954, Baba Johani dreamed that he was wrapped in a white cloth (muchenje).
This was understood as a sign concerning his burial and the garments of the faithful.
CHAPTER 15: TEACHING ON WHITE GARMENTS (04/10/1955) — On 4 October 1955, Baba Johani taught concerning the wearing of white garments.
He said, White is the color of purity, of angels, and of those who stand before the throne.
From that day, the white robes became the mark of his followers.
CHAPTER 16: TEACHING ON RESURRECTION (15/03/1955) — On 15 March 1955, Baba Johani taught concerning the resurrection of the dead.
He said, Death is not the end. The body returns to dust, but the spirit rises.
Those who keep the commandments will rise to eternal life.
CHAPTER 17: THE BATTLE AT PORT ELIZABETH (25/12/1957) — On Christmas Day, 25 December 1957, there was conflict among the people at Port Elizabeth.
Baba Johani spoke words of peace and judgment, separating the faithful from the rebellious.
CHAPTER 18: SEEKING WHAT FINISHES PEOPLE (31/03/1957) — On 31 March 1957, Baba Johani sought to understand what destroys people.
He taught that sin, disobedience, and departure from the commandments are the true killers of the soul.
CHAPTER 19: LEAVING PORT ELIZABETH (01/01/1958) — On 1 January 1958, the event called 'Zvakataurwa naBaba Johane pamusoro pehondo yenyika paPort Elizabeth' was recorded.
The Masowe history records this as a significant transition in the work at Port Elizabeth.
CHAPTER 20: AT PORT ELIZABETH (14 JULY 1959) — On 14 July 1959, Baba Johani was present at Port Elizabeth continuing his ministry.
Many teachings and healings occurred during this period.
CHAPTER 21: LEAVING BAY FOR JORDAN (16/10/1959) — On 16 October 1959, Baba Johani left Bay (Port Elizabeth) heading toward 'Jordan' (Botswana, Zimbabwe).
He said, The work must spread to all the nations.
CHAPTER 22: TEACHING ON BURIAL (03/03/1966) — On 3 March 1966, Baba Johani taught concerning where one should be buried when they die.
He gave instructions about proper burial according to the faith.
CHAPTER 23: THE SPIRIT IN THE BODY (23/08/1966) — On 23 August 1966, Baba Johani spoke concerning the spirit of a person while still in the body.
He taught about the relationship between the physical and spiritual nature of humanity.
CHAPTER 24: THE SCROLL OF JOSHUA (14/08/1967) — On 14 August 1967, while at Mutare, Baba Johani received a vision concerning Joshua chapter 1 verse 8.
He had gone to the mountain to fast and pray, seeking what would save his people.
In a dream, he went to heaven and was given the scroll of Joshua 1:8.
It was revealed: Your people will be saved by the words in Joshua 1:8. They must keep the words of the Ten Commandments and the words of the prophets.
Those who keep what is written in the Holy Bible will be saved. Their names will be found written in the Book of Life.
CHAPTER 25: THE VISION OF THE NEW LAND (23/11/1967) — On 23 November 1967, Baba Johani was shown a new land, called 'nyika itsva.'
He was instructed to send workers even to overseas countries, yet the root of the work remained in the South.
He said, This new land will become the new home of truth and of the people of a new covenant.
CHAPTER 26: TEACHING OF WORKERS AND WOMEN (01/01/1968) — On 1 January 1968, Baba Johani taught the workers (vabasa) and the women of the church (madzimayi echirangano).
He gave instructions concerning order, service, and faithfulness.
CHAPTER 27: CONTINUED TEACHING OF WOMEN (15/01/1968) — On 15 January 1968, Baba Johani continued teaching the women of the church.
He said, Women are the pillars of the home and the keepers of tradition.
CHAPTER 28: THE DREAM OF THREE PEOPLE (28/02/1973) — On 28 February 1973, Baba Johani dreamed of seeing three people.
This was understood as a sign of transition, signaling the approaching end of his earthly journey.
CHAPTER 29: SPEAKING TO MAI MANGWENDEZA (12/03/1973) — On 12 March 1973, Baba Johani spoke to Mai Mangwendeza concerning her grave.
He spoke as one who knew the road ahead, preparing his people for what was to come.
CHAPTER 30: THE DAY THE VOICE FELL SILENT (13 SEPTEMBER 1973) — On 13 September 1973, Baba Johani departed from the body.
Silence came upon the people like a deep mist.
There was weeping, yet not without understanding, for many said, He has finished what he was sent to do.
The land did not rejoice, nor did it rebel; it bore witness.
CHAPTER 31: AFTER THE DEPARTURE — After his passing, divisions arose among those who followed him.
Some sought authority, others remembrance, others truth.
His words were gathered, his deeds retold, and his path measured.
Gandanzara and the places of his prayer became points of return.
And his death was not counted as loss, but as completion, for a messenger does not remain once the message is delivered.
CHAPTER 32: THE LOCATIONS OF HIS MINISTRY — These are the places where Baba Johani walked and worked:
GANDANZARA — His birthplace in 1914, at the homestead of Mugwambi.
NORTON — Where the Holy Spirit descended upon him at Gomo reMarimba (Marimba Hill), at Chipukutu by the Hunyani River.
NYAMWEDA — Where he sang and healed, and where the blind policeman was restored.
CHIVERO — Where he continued his ministry of healing.
WEDZA (MATSINE) — Where his staff, book, and hoe were taken from him.
MARONDERA — Where pots and shovels moved by themselves in the prison.
HARARE — Where he was tried at the Anglican Cathedral and released.
RUSAPE — A place of both suffering and ministry.
BUHERA — One of the seven places of his early persecution.
ZAKA, MASVINGO — Where he flew like an eagle to rescue the young man.
BULAWAYO — Where he found rest and the church grew.
GWERU — Where he met Muparidzi George and left his carpenter tools.
PORT ELIZABETH, SOUTH AFRICA — Where the church flourished among migrants from 1951.
NAIROBI, KENYA — Where he spoke prophecies recorded in the scrolls.
ARUSHA, TANZANIA — Where he spoke while traveling through East Africa.
CHAPTER 33: THE WITNESS OF THE SCROLL — Thus the birth of Baba Johani was announced by signs, and his death sealed by fulfillment.
He came marked, he walked set apart, and he departed having spoken.
Let this scroll stand as witness.
Not to glorify flesh, but to testify that heaven still appoints, visits, and releases.
And let the reader understand: Birth is a calling. Life is a mission. Death is a return.
So ends the Scroll of BABA JOHANI — LIFE EVENTS AND SACRED MISSION.
